images.caption: Figure 1:Comparison with previous agent benchmarks and WildClawBench.(a)Prior benchmarks evaluate short-horizon, single-step tasks with toy APIs in controlled sandboxes, whereas(b)WildClawBench evaluates long-horizon multimodal workflows with real tools in open-world environments.(c)The benchmark spans six categories and is compatible with multiple agent harnesses.(d)A summary of key differences across environment, task horizon, tool use, and evaluation.
Figure 2:Representative WildClawBench tasks.One example per category (a–f), each showing the user prompt, an illustration of the expected workflow, and the evaluation checklist used for grading.

Figure 3:WildClawBench statistics.(Left, Middle)Task distribution across modality and language, broken down by the six categories.(Right)Average per-task time budget (seconds) by category, ranging from 510s (Social Interaction) to 1200s (Code Intelligence).

Figure 4:Four-stage curation pipeline.(1) Authoringof candidate tasks across six categories.(2) Pilotconstruction of reference answers and rubrics for LLM-as-a-Judge grading.(3) Human and Model Filteringvia transcript audit and a discriminability threshold (maxi≠j⁡Δi​j≥0.2\max_{i\neq j}\Delta_{ij}\geq 0.2).(4) Refinementof prompts, rubrics, and grading logic, yielding the final 60-task benchmark.

Table 2:Main results on WildClawBench under the OpenClaw harness.Time (Minutes) and cost (USD) are per-task averages. Score is reported in %. The Overall column is a task-count-weighted average of the Multimodal (26 tasks) and Pure Text (34 tasks) columns.↑\uparrow/↓\downarrowdenote whether higher or lower is better. For an analysis of performance variance across repeated independent runs, please refer to Table10.
Table 3:Comparison across harnesses.Time (minutes) and cost (USD) are per-task averages; Score is reported in %.↑\uparrow/↓\downarrowdenote whether higher or lower is better.

Figure 5:Left:domain-specific strengths across models.Middle:score scaling with execution-time budget. Scores are normalized by each model’s standard setting (1×1\timescompute time, set to100%100\%). Dots are individual model-budget results; the curve shows the fitted scaling trend.Right:absolute performance under Half, Standard, and Double budgets for each model.

Table 4:Effect of skill augmentation on OpenClaw, by category.Time (minutes) and cost (USD) are per-task averages over 60 tasks; Score is reported in %.
Each+Skillvalue shows its signed change from theBaserow above.Greenmarks an improvement (higher Score, or lower Time/Cost) andredmarks a regression.↑\uparrow/↓\downarrowdenote whether higher or lower is better.
Table 5:GPT 5.4 thinking-mode comparison under a fixed task budget.Time (minutes) and cost (USD) are per-task averages.

Table 6:Tool-use behavior across models.Average tool calls per trajectory aggregated over all 60 OpenClaw tasks per model:web==web_search++web_fetch;author==edit++write.Totalcovers all tool calls, including categories not shown.

header: Appendix DSkills Used in Category-Level Ablation

images.caption: Figure 6:Failure-mode breakdown for 300 OpenClaw runs across five models. Panel (a) is the outcome view with mutually exclusive failure outcomes per failed run. Panel (b) is the process view (multi-label heatmap): cells count failed runs among the 60 evaluated tasks for each signal; signals can co-occur within the same failure.

Table 10:Variance of repeated runs on OpenClaw.Scores are reported as Mean±\pmStandard Deviation (%) across three independent runs. The small standard deviations indicate stable performance across trials.

Table 11:Human-GPT Agreement Case Study.Raw scores from two independent human evaluators compared with the GPT 5.4 judge across five sampled tasks. The rightmost column displays the GPT 5.4 score, with the deviation from the Human Average appended in parentheses.

header: Appendix KWord Cloud Analysis of Prompts and Trajectories

images.caption:
Figure 7:Word cloud of all task prompts in WildClawBench after filtering common stopwords and low-
information instruction tokens. Larger words indicate higher frequency across benchmark
prompts. The visualization shows that WildClawBench covers a diverse set of task themes,
including multimodal image and video processing, web search, API usage, arXiv and paper
analysis, GitHub repositories, webpage generation, and structured document creation.

Figure 8:Word cloud of Claude Opus 4.6 assistant trajectories on WildClawBench. The corpus is
constructed from assistant-side messages in the recordedchat.jsonlfiles,
including responses, reasoning content, and tool-call arguments, while excluding user
prompts and tool results. Compared with the prompt-level word cloud, this trajectory-level
visualization emphasizes the model’s execution behavior, including Python programming, image
manipulation, search, screenshot generation, repository operations, grid-based visual
reasoning, and iterative solution construction.
